mappings.rationaleAI: Typology of societies from hunting-gathering through industrial directly maps to types of cultures (B), with theoretical frameworks relevant to diverse theories of culture (A).

Survey from hunter-gatherer origins through the present, covering Neolithic Revolution and cultures of primitive societies (B.1), origin of civilized societies (B.2), and modern industrial civilization (B.3).
Complex societies characterized by state formation and urbanization, covering theories of origin and evolution of civilized societies (B.2) and development of modern industrial civilization (B.3).
Behavioral and emotional patterns constituting individual adjustment, directly relevant to the relationship between culture and personality (A.2).
Social behaviors, norms, and customs encompassing definitions of culture (A.1), cultural relativism and ethnocentrism (A.3), cultural adaptation and change (A.4), and cultural patterns (A.5).
Cultivation of soil and livestock tracing from Neolithic domestication, relevant to horticultural societies (B.1.b) and settled agriculturist cultures (B.1.d).

Global transition from hunter-gatherer to sedentary agriculture, marking the shift from primitive cultures (B.1) to settled agricultural societies (B.1.d) and the emergence of civilized societies (B.2).
Scientific study of humanity encompassing definitions of culture (A.1), cultural relativism (A.3), and ethnographic study of primitive and nonurban societies (B.1).
Prehistoric period of lithic tool use, spanning hunter-gatherer societies (B.1.a) and encompassing cultures of nomadic and settled peoples.
Historical period defined by the shift to information-technology-centered economies, directly constituting the development of modern industrial/post-industrial civilization and mass society (B.3).
Intensifying global interdependence driving cultural adaptation and change (A.4) and the development of modern mass society (B.3).
Transmission of knowledge through speech and performance, the predominant cultural medium of primitive and nonurban societies (B.1).
Dense non-agricultural settlements with complex governance, marking the emergence of civilized societies (B.2) and the development of modern industrial civilization (B.3).
Demographic shift from rural to urban residence, driving the origin of civilized societies (B.2) and characterizing modern industrial civilization (B.3).
Small rural settlement historically associated with subsistence agriculture, representing cultures of peasants and settled agriculturists (B.1.d).
Agricultural management of livestock tracing to Neolithic domestication, directly representing cultures of pastoralists and herdsmen (B.1.c).
Historical period from the 19th century defined by industrialization, nationalism, and globalization, constituting the development of modern industrial civilization and mass society (B.3).
Inventor of the European movable-type printing press, catalyzing cultural change through information technology (A.4) and contributing to the development of modern civilization (B.3).
Cultural and religious analysis in Totem and Taboo and Civilization and Its Discontents directly addresses the relationship between culture and personality (A.2).
